metadata.title: Understanding Federal and State Taxes
metadata.subtitle: How Tax Systems Impact Your Financial Life

content.title: Understanding Federal and State Taxes
content.subtitle: How Tax Systems Impact Your Financial Life

content.label: The Challenge
content.question:
Mia in California and Liam in Texas both earn $70,000 annually. After federal and state taxes, Mia takes home $51,100 while Liam takes home $54,900 with no state income tax—but Texas has significantly higher property taxes than California.

How does {{STATE_NAME}}'s tax structure compare to states you might consider living in?

slides.speakerNotes: Read the comparison scenario featuring {{STATE_NAME}}'s {{STATE_INCOME_TAX_RATE}}% income tax rate vs. the national average. Ask the discussion question: 'How does {{STATE_NAME}}'s tax structure compare to states you might consider living in?' Allow 2-3 students to share where they've lived or might consider moving. This introduces the core concept that tax structures vary widely by state and significantly impact take-home income.

objectives.description: Identify the differences between federal and state taxes and understand how each affects your income.
Understand how progressive tax brackets work and why you don't pay the top rate on all your income.
Recognize payroll taxes (FICA) and understand their purpose in funding Social Security and Medicare.
Analyze how tax structures vary across states and how this impacts financial decisions.

terms.definition: A tax system where higher-income earners pay a higher percentage of their income in taxes.
terms.example: Federal income tax: 10% on first $11K, 12% on next $33K, 22% on amounts above that.

terms.definition: An income range that is subject to a specific tax rate in a progressive tax system.
terms.example: Only income WITHIN each bracket is taxed at that rate—not your entire income!

slides.speakerNotes: Review the 5 key terms on this and the next slide. Draw a progressive tax bracket visual on the board showing how income is divided into brackets like a staircase. List the 7 states with no income tax (Alaska, Florida, Nevada, South Dakota, Tennessee, Texas, Wyoming) and discuss how they fund services differently than {{STATE_NAME}}—often through higher property taxes, sales taxes, or resource-based revenue. Clarify FICA clearly: 6.2% Social Security + 1.45% Medicare = 7.65% total that comes out of every paycheck.

terms.definition: Employment taxes funding Social Security (6.2%) and Medicare (1.45%) — totaling 7.65%.
terms.example: Your employer matches your contribution, so 15.3% total goes to these programs.

terms.definition: The portion of your paycheck your employer keeps to pay taxes on your behalf throughout the year.
terms.example: Prevents a large tax bill in April—taxes are paid as you earn income.

layoutData.title: Taxes Pay for Public Services We All Use
paragraphs: When you pay taxes, you're contributing to a wide range of programs and infrastructure that benefit society:
bulletPoints: Education: Public schools, colleges, student aid programs
Public Safety: Police, fire departments, emergency services
layoutData.keyPoint: 💡 Understanding where tax money goes helps you participate in policy discussions about how it should be spent.

leftPanel.title: The Common Misconception
paragraphs: Many people think: "If I'm in the 22% bracket, I pay 22% on ALL my income."
This is wrong! You only pay each rate on income within that specific bracket.

rightColumn.title: National Comparison
items: States with No Income Tax: 7 states

content.headerTitle: Why State Taxes Vary So Much

scenario.name: Mia (California) vs. Liam (Texas)
paragraphs: Both earn $70,000 annually. Let's compare their situations:

leftPanel.title: What FICA Funds
paragraphs: Social Security (6.2%): Retirement benefits, disability income, survivor benefits for families.
Medicare (1.45%): Healthcare coverage for those 65+ and certain disabled individuals.
Your employer matches your contribution, meaning 15.3% total goes to these programs for every dollar you earn.
🔄 Unlike income tax, FICA is a flat rate on all wages (SS has a cap at ~$168,600 in 2024).

slides.speakerNotes:
COMMON MISCONCEPTIONS TO ADDRESS:

1. 'Moving to a higher tax bracket means ALL my income is taxed at that rate' → CLARIFY: Only the income WITHIN each bracket is taxed at that rate. If you earn $50,000, only $5,275 is taxed at 22%—not all $50,000. This is the most important misconception to correct!

2. 'States with no income tax are always cheaper than {{STATE_NAME}}' → CLARIFY: No-income-tax states often have higher property taxes, sales taxes, or fees. Total tax burden depends on individual circumstances. Compare {{STATE_NAME}}'s {{STATE_PROPERTY_TAX_AVG}}% property tax to Texas's ~1.8%.

3. 'FICA taxes are optional' → CLARIFY: FICA (Social Security + Medicare) is mandatory for all wage earners. It funds programs you'll likely use in retirement. This is not optional and cannot be opted out of.

Address these proactively during discussion, especially #1 which causes significant confusion about how tax brackets work.

content.question:
How might understanding different state tax systems influence your decisions about where to live and work after graduation?

What factors beyond taxes would you consider?

content.headerTitle: Why This Matters for Your Future

layoutData.title: Tax Knowledge = Better Financial Decisions
paragraphs: Understanding how taxes work helps you in multiple areas of life:
bulletPoints: Job Offers: Compare after-tax income, not just salary numbers
Budgeting: Know your actual take-home pay before committing to expenses
Location: Consider total tax burden when choosing where to live
Retirement: Pre-tax contributions (401k) reduce your taxable income now
Side Income: Self-employment means paying both halves of FICA (15.3%)
Investments: Different investment income has different tax treatment
layoutData.keyPoint: 🎯 The financial decisions you make now—informed by tax knowledge—compound over your career.

content.question:
What's the trade-off between states with high taxes and more government services vs. states with low taxes and fewer services?

Which would you prefer and why?

rightColumn.title: Regressive Taxes
items: Lower earners pay higher % of income

questions.question: If someone is in the "22% tax bracket," does that mean all their income is taxed at 22%? Explain why or why not.
What two programs do FICA taxes fund, and what percentage of your paycheck goes to each?
Why might someone in Texas still end up paying similar total taxes to someone in California?
Why is sales tax considered a "regressive" tax even though everyone pays the same rate?

slides.speakerNotes:
ANSWER KEY:

Q1: No, being in the 22% tax bracket does NOT mean all income is taxed at 22%. Only the income WITHIN each bracket is taxed at that rate. For example, if you earn $50,000: the first $11,000 is taxed at 10% ($1,100), the next $33,725 is taxed at 12% ($4,047), and only the last $5,275 is taxed at 22% ($1,161). Total tax is $6,308, which is an effective rate of 12.6%—not 22%.

Q2: FICA taxes fund Social Security (6.2%) and Medicare (1.45%), totaling 7.65% of your paycheck. Social Security provides retirement benefits, disability income, and survivor benefits. Medicare provides healthcare coverage for those 65+ and certain disabled individuals. Your employer matches this 7.65%, so 15.3% total goes to these programs.

Q3: Someone in Texas might still pay similar total taxes to someone in California because while Texas has no state income tax (0%), it has significantly higher property taxes (~1.8% vs. California's 0.77%) and potentially higher sales taxes. The total tax burden depends on individual circumstances—homeowners, especially those with expensive properties, may pay more in Texas despite no income tax.

Q4: Sales tax is considered regressive because lower-income people spend a higher percentage of their income on taxable goods, so sales tax takes a larger share of their total income compared to wealthy people who save/invest more. Even though everyone pays the same rate (e.g., {{STATE_SALES_TAX}}%), it represents a bigger burden for those with less income.

takeaways.title: Progressive System
takeaways.description: Federal tax rates increase with income, but only income in each bracket is taxed at that rate

takeaways.title: States Vary Widely
takeaways.description: From 0% to 13%+ income tax—but no-tax states often have higher property/sales taxes

takeaways.title: FICA is Flat
takeaways.description: Everyone pays 7.65% for Social Security and Medicare, regardless of income level

takeaways.title: Consider Total Picture
takeaways.description: When comparing locations, look at ALL taxes: income, property, sales, and fees

main.title: Analyze Tax Fairness Scenarios
main.description: You'll evaluate three different taxpayers and determine which tax system (progressive or regressive) would be more appropriate for each situation. Scenarios include: • John: Entry-level worker ($25K) • Samantha: Mid-career professional ($80K) • Andrew: Business owner ($500K) Consider: ability to pay, impact on financial situation, and benefits received from public services.

content.tagline: Building Financial Futures, One Lesson at a Time
